html:
XII

‚ÄúReally, Joanna, you ought to treat me better. You know I‚Äôm staying in New York just on account of you!‚Äù

‚ÄúHow do you want me to treat you, Peter?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, hang it all. Why can‚Äôt you be nicer to me? When Brian comes to see Sylvia she runs to meet him, puts her arms about his neck.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut Sylvia and Brian are engaged. You and I are just friends.‚Äù

‚ÄúJust friends! Joanna, have a heart. What do you think I spend all my spare time with you for? You know how I feel.‚Äù

Joanna raised a slim, protesting hand. ‚ÄúNone of that, Peter! You come to see me because both of us are interested in the same things. Each of us is going to be an artist in different ways. What other girl is there in New York who would let you talk to her about the joys of surgery?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat other girl would want me to?‚Äù

Joanna, looking at the long brown figure lying full length on the grass, thought it highly improbable that any other girl would. She had seen other girls in the company of Peter, and watched quite without jealousy their ways with him. She rather prided herself on her own aloofness from such tactics. Of course, some day she might let Peter talk to her about things other than work and art, and she might answer him, but at present the big things of life must be arranged. Love was an after consideration, she felt, and as far as she knew she meant it.

It was a Saturday afternoon in July and the two were in Van Cortlandt Park. Peter was to go to school in Philadelphia in the fall, and it was important for him to earn as much money as possible for his expenses. He might have gone with a group of other boys to one of the watering places and worked in a hotel. But that took him too far away from Joanna. Ragtime was coming into vogue then, and Peter proved himself an adept at it. The butcher shop was of course a thing long since of the past.

‚ÄúHere‚Äôs where I put my gift of strumming to some use,‚Äù he laughed to Joanna. ‚ÄúYou ought to see how glad they were to take me on at that cabaret.‚Äù

‚ÄúI hope you won‚Äôt learn anything you shouldn‚Äôt in that atmosphere,‚Äù she had answered primly.

‚ÄúOh, of course I won‚Äôt,‚Äù he returned, thinking how amazed she would be if she ever looked down from her pinnacle long enough to understand what life really was. He would have liked her to see that cabaret with its jostling crowds and blaring lights, and the host of noisy good-hearted dancing girls. He tried to give her some description of it. But Joanna turned away.

‚ÄúMen and women are like that, just the same,‚Äù he protested. ‚ÄúEverybody isn‚Äôt living on the mountaintops like you, Janna. I can‚Äôt live there of my own accord myself. That‚Äôs why I haunt you so because you do keep me on the heights, dear.‚Äù She liked that.

‚ÄúBut just the same,‚Äù he resumed, rolling over on the short grass like a lithe handsome animal, ‚Äúall the big things of life smack of the earth. Your poet has to eat, or he can‚Äôt write poetry. Well, so does the commonest laboring-man. The queen has children, in agony, Janna, just like the poorest charwoman. And love is the‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe driving force for both of them.‚Äù He mused a little. ‚ÄúLove is the most natural and ordinary thing in the world.‚Äù

But Joanna didn‚Äôt believe that. ‚ÄúLove is a wonderful, rare thing, very beautiful, very sweet, but you can do without it.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot much you can‚Äôt. Better not try it, Joanna. You have to found your life on love, then you can do all these other things.‚Äù

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt talk like a silly, Peter. You know perfectly well that for a woman love usually means a household of children, the getting of a thousand meals, picking up laundry, no time to herself for meditation, or reading or‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúDancing! That‚Äôs through poor management. Marry a man who understands you, Janna, and he‚Äôll see that you have time for anything you want. Where is such a man? Behold him!‚Äù He struck his chest dramatically.

‚ÄúPeter Bye! How you talk!‚Äù

‚ÄúAll right, I‚Äôll choose something else. Tell me why is it that though I‚Äôve elected to stay in New York in all this hot weather just to be at your side, I see less of you than at any time since I‚Äôve been coming to your house.‚Äù

‚ÄúDoes seem queer, doesn‚Äôt it? It must be because I have so much work to do. I am taking extra singing lessons from Brailoff now. And my dancing takes up a lot of my time; my classes come at such inconvenient hours, 7:30 to 10:00 three times a week.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat is bad. Funny time to give dancing lessons. Where‚Äôd you say you took them?‚Äù

‚ÄúAt Bertully‚Äôs.‚Äù

‚ÄúBertully‚Äôs! That‚Äôs in Twenty-ninth Street, isn‚Äôt it? How‚Äôd you ever make it? I didn‚Äôt suppose a colored girl got a chance to stick her nose in there.‚Äù

‚ÄúShe wouldn‚Äôt ordinarily. Bertully refused Helena Arnold last year. ‚ÄòI‚Äôm sorry, Mees, but the white Americans like not to study with the brown Americans. Vair seely, but so. I am a poor man, I must follow the weeshes of my clients!‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù Joanna shrugged her shoulders, spread her hands.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre a born impersonator, Jan. I can see that little Frenchman now. How‚Äôd you ever get in, then?‚Äù

‚ÄúHelena and I went back this year and asked if he would take a separate class of colored girls, if we got it up for him. He was very decent, said he‚Äôd be glad to. So we got up a class of eight, he only asked for six. Of course, we had to take his hours.‚Äù

‚ÄúWho are in it besides you and Helena?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, all our crowd.‚Äù She named the daughters of several prominent colored men, a physician, a lawyer, a journalist, a real-estate man among them. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs Gertrude Moseley, Vera and Alice Manning, Elizabeth Beckett, Sylvia, Helena, and I.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs seven.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, yes, Sylvia meant to ask Maggie Ellersley.‚Äù

‚ÄúH‚Äôm, she had other things in her head without bothering about fancy dancing, hadn‚Äôt she? Funny how she went off and married without telling any of us about it, wasn‚Äôt it?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Joanna uneasily.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôd have thought she‚Äôd have let old Phil in on it. I wonder if they had a falling out of any kind! Philip seemed rather hard hit when he heard the news.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot a bit of it. Why should he be?‚Äù Joanna spoke stoutly. But her tone belied her convictions. She hadn‚Äôt forgotten Philip‚Äôs expression the day Sylvia had come rushing in with the astounding news:

‚ÄúWhat do you think? I just met Mrs.¬ÝEllersley. Maggie‚Äôs married‚ÅÝ‚Äîmarried‚ÅÝ‚Äîthink of it! She ran away with that man at her house, that Mr.¬ÝNeal. And they‚Äôre going to live in Philadelphia.‚Äù

Philip‚Äôs haggard face had turned a trifle more wan, Joanna had thought. ‚ÄúHas she written to you, Sylvia?‚Äù he asked her quickly.

‚ÄúNot a word. I can‚Äôt imagine why she said nothing to me about it. She must have planned it for ages. If that isn‚Äôt the funniest!‚Äù

Later Joanna heard Philip asking his mother if she were sure she had given him all the mail that had come for him while he was in Philadelphia. Still later he had announced his intention of teaching summer school in South Carolina.

‚ÄúFellow whose place I‚Äôm going to fill is sick. They‚Äôve been at me a long time to come. I think I ought to go, father. It will give me a chance to see the South.‚Äù

Joanna‚Äôs throat constricted a little at the thought of Philip‚Äôs look, his general listlessness. She wished she hadn‚Äôt written that letter. Though that couldn‚Äôt have brought about the marriage. People don‚Äôt arrange to be married over night. As Sylvia said, it must have been on Maggie‚Äôs mind long since. And then, anyway, Philip couldn‚Äôt really have cared for a girl like Maggie.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt believe Philip was the least bit interested in Maggie,‚Äù she voiced her thought to Peter. ‚ÄúWell, anyway, Mr.¬ÝBye, that‚Äôs why my company is so scarce. Goodness, what are you frowning about?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôm mad to think you swallowed that Frenchman‚Äôs insult. To think of your taking lessons from him after that!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut, Peter, he didn‚Äôt insult us. He can‚Äôt help this stupid prejudice. ‚ÄòIn my country, Mademoiselle Mar√©chal,‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe always calls me that‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äòyou‚Äôd be an honor to any class.‚Äô He says I‚Äôve got a great future. That if there‚Äôs anything that will break down prejudice it will be equality or perhaps even superiority on the part of colored people in the arts. And I agree with him.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut to be set apart like that!‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat do I care?‚Äù asked Joanna, the practical. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve got to take life as you find it, Peter. The way I figure it is this. If all I needed to get on the stage was the mastery of a difficult step, I‚Äôd get there, wouldn‚Äôt I? For somehow, sometime, I‚Äôd learn how to overcome that difficulty.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou bet you would.‚Äù

‚ÄúVery well, then. Now my problem is how to master, how to get around prejudice. It is an awful nuisance; in some parts of this country it is more than a nuisance, it‚Äôs a veritable menace. Philip says he‚Äôs going to change all that some day. First, I‚Äôm going to get my training up to the last notch, then I‚Äôm going to watch for an opportunity and squeeze in.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou‚Äôll never get it.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, yes, I will. Some white people are kind, some of them are so truly artistic that they‚Äôll put themselves to great trouble for the sake of art. Look at Bertully. It works him much harder than it does us to hold those extra classes.‚Äù

‚ÄúBertully‚Äôs one man in a thousand. Besides, he‚Äôs a foreigner. Where‚Äôll you find a white American like that?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou blessed pessimist. I know of people like that already. That‚Äôs how Helena Arnold got to Bertully in the first place. A Miss Sharples‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhy, they‚Äôre the people your Aunt Susan works for, aren‚Äôt they? Your aunt told Miss Sharples about Helena, and Miss Sharples took her, herself, to Bertully.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat was awfully decent, I must say. Of course, the Sharples are Philadelphia Quaker stock. Not that that makes much difference. The white Byes were Quakers, and see how they left us stranded, though my father told me old black Joshua Bye practically coined them their money. Not many people like those Sharples.‚Äù

‚ÄúThere doesn‚Äôt need to be. The point is there‚Äôs one. Miss Sharples‚Äô family, by the way, may have been Quakers, but there‚Äôs nothing Quakerish about her. Helena says she goes with the Greenwich Village group all the time, and for all their craziness, they‚Äôve got some mighty big ideas.‚Äù

‚ÄúCan‚Äôt get anything to eat, if you‚Äôre colored, down in their dinky old restaurants.‚Äù

‚ÄúAwful, isn‚Äôt it? Well, we‚Äôll let some other colored person pound away at that side of it. Me, I‚Äôm going to break into art. The public wants novelty, and I want fame, I‚Äôve got to have it, Peter.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou talk about going on the stage as though you had a signed contract in your hand. How‚Äôll you get the stage-presence?‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm to go on a recital tour next fall among colored people. I‚Äôm used to singing in the choir. If I can stand before them I can stand before any audience in the world.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, we are mighty critical.‚Äù

‚ÄúI should say so. Get up, Peter Bye. We‚Äôve got to go home.‚Äù

They started on the long trip back.

‚ÄúBut see here, Joanna,‚Äù Peter pleaded when they reached the house, ‚Äúyou will give me a little more time, won‚Äôt you? I don‚Äôt have to work in the morning, you know. And I don‚Äôt work Wednesday nights. Promise me that, won‚Äôt you?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Joanna, her heart warming to his glowing beauty. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll remember this summer, Peter, the last before we go off trying our wings for further flights.‚Äù

That was an enchanted season. Peter used to call for her in the morning, and the two would go off exploring. Joanna liked the foreign quarters, but she had never cared to stand around too long in those teeming, exotic streets. She was too conspicuous, attracted too many inquiring glances. With Peter she felt safe to stand for long moments watching the children play, to enter queer dark shops, to taste strange messes. Sometimes she spoke to the women about their dresses, their headgear. One Spanish woman, grown used to the sight of this dark American girl and the good-looking boy at her side, took them into her quarters one day and showed Joanna how she dressed her hair. Another time she taught her an intricate Spanish dance.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm going to do a dance representing all the nations, some day,‚Äù Joanna told Peter.

They planned for Wednesday nights very carefully at first, but gradually as the torrid weather increased, Joanna‚Äôs desire for the theater and other indoor forms of amusement yielded to the desire to be cool at any cost. Central Park claimed them then, and later Morningside, since it was just a few moments‚Äô stroll from the Marshalls‚Äô new house.

Morningside was usually crowded. The seats were always taken when they arrived.

‚ÄúI wonder what time the people come,‚Äù Joanna murmured. But they didn‚Äôt mind. The grass, the sloping hillside, was good enough for them. Joanna would sit down, her dainty summer dress spread around her, her splendidly poised head turned at first so she could see the passersby. She was forever studying types, and eyed them with a grave deliberation.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôll get your head knocked off yet, Joanna,‚Äù Peter would remonstrate, ‚Äústaring at people so.‚Äù

He liked it better when later on in the evening she turned toward the slope of the hill and looked down at the city, laughing in its myriad twinkling lights. Her face at that time took on a grave wistfulness which he could not analyze. Joanna herself could not define the feeling which prompted that expression.

Peter, leaning on his elbow, would lie beside her, his curly black head bent toward her, one slender brown hand touching her dress ever so lightly. He would have given the world to believe she was thinking about him, but he knew she was not. He would have been astounded if he could have dreamed of the maze of her thoughts. Joanna was really most human at moments like these. Through her mind was floating a series of little detached pictures. She saw a glittering stage, Peter, herself, some little children. She felt a hazy, nebulous, mystical joy.

Peter adored her at moments like these, but he was afraid of her, too.

One night she astonished him. ‚ÄúPeter,‚Äù she said suddenly, ‚Äúsit up. So. I‚Äôm tired. I‚Äôve had a hard day. Do you mind if I rest my head on your shoulder?‚Äù

Would he mind if she offered him a king‚Äôs estate?

He was too ecstatic, too‚ÅÝ‚Äîyes‚ÅÝ‚Äîscared, to speak. He sat as she directed, he stretched his thin tense arm around her fine young body. He even put up one hand and pressed her head closer against his shoulder, touched her hair, let his fingers trail ever so lightly over her cheek. Joanna in his arms! Joanna!

She felt him trembling. ‚ÄúAm I too heavy, Peter?‚Äù

He could hardly articulate, but she heard his ardent ‚Äúno‚Äù and moved imperceptibly closer.

His breath stirred her thick, dark hair. He let it caress his chin. Its soft heaviness was a revelation to him, a rapture.

She lay so quietly against him he thought she must be asleep. So he whispered, ‚ÄúAre you asleeep, Joanna?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù she whispered back, ‚Äúonly very, very tired.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, Joanna, Joanna,‚Äù he breathed, ‚Äúbe tired forever.‚Äù

Somewhere out of the heavenly silence, a girl‚Äôs voice, a foreign voice, broke into song high and shrill. Russian, Peter thought. It was just a snatch, poignant and sweet, that died away leaving a faint lingering sadness.

She put her head back then. She opened her dark eyes and looked full into his.

Their lips were so near, so near. In a second he had pressed his against hers, briefly yet with passion. She sat up and drew a little away from him, dazed. But he put his arms around her and held her close. Presently they walked home, speechless. When they came to an arc-light, they looked at each other‚Äôs faces, eager to study and to reveal these new selves. Their glances met and clung with a sweet enchantment. Something leaped, something fluttered within their hearts, like a fettered, struggling wing. And it was beautifully, it was magically, first love!
